meaning: I have no idea / no clue.
origin: Modern Hebrew idiom; musag = concept.

meaning: See you / goodbye (literally 'to see each other').
origin: Modern Hebrew standard farewell; reflexive form of l'rot (to see).

meaning: Good morning.
origin: Standard morning greeting.

meaning: Good night.
origin: Standard night farewell.

meaning: Many thanks (literally 'much thanks').
origin: Standard Hebrew thanks; raba = many/much (feminine).

meaning: Please / you're welcome (used both ways).
origin: Modern Hebrew formula; literally 'in request.'

meaning: Excuse me / sorry / pardon.
origin: Hebrew slicha = forgiveness; productive in everyday apology and attention-getting.

meaning: Look favorably / smile upon — 'find grace in your eyes.'
origin: Biblical idiom: matza ḥen be-eineiha (he found favor in her eyes).

meaning: Finally / at last (literally 'end-end').
origin: Modern Hebrew reduplication; reduplication for emphasis is common.

meaning: Not okay / not right.
origin: Mirror of 'beseder' (okay); literally 'not in order.'

meaning: How's it going (literally 'how does it go').
origin: Modern Hebrew casual greeting.

meaning: Day-day / daily / every day.
origin: Reduplication for distributive meaning.

meaning: No such things — used as denial/rejection.
origin: Modern Hebrew dismissive idiom.

meaning: So what / what now.
origin: Modern Hebrew dismissive/inquisitive.

meaning: What's up / how are things (literally 'what matters').
origin: Modern Hebrew greeting; inyanim = matters/affairs.

meaning: Come on, chaos! — said when accepting that things will be messy.
origin: Modern Hebrew slang combining Arabic-derived yalla (let's go) and Russian/Yiddish-derived balagan (chaos).

meaning: Onion — but idiomatically 'in the shade' (b'tsel) or 'small thing.' Note: same surface for two unrelated meanings.
origin: Hebrew homograph; context distinguishes.

meaning: I have no strength / I can't be bothered.
origin: Modern Hebrew exhaustion idiom.

meaning: It's not hard.
origin: Modern Hebrew reassurance.

meaning: All good / all the best — farewell/well-wishing.
origin: Modern Hebrew farewell.

meaning: With success / good luck (literally 'in success').
origin: Standard Hebrew well-wishing for ventures.

meaning: Complete recovery — said to the sick.
origin: Traditional Jewish blessing; refuah = healing, shleimah = complete.

meaning: With God's help — pious phrase before mentioning a future event.
origin: Religious Hebrew phrase, parallel to Arabic in shā' Allāh.

meaning: Blessed is the Name (of God) — 'thank God' / 'praise God.'
origin: Religious Hebrew gratitude phrase, parallel to Arabic alhamdulillah.

meaning: If God wills — pious conditional.
origin: Religious Hebrew, parallel to Arabic in shā' Allāh.

meaning: To life! — toast.
origin: Traditional Jewish toast; le-ḥayyim = 'to lives' (always plural).

meaning: Happy holiday.
origin: Standard Hebrew holiday greeting; ḥag = pilgrimage festival.

meaning: Good year — Rosh Hashanah greeting.
origin: Standard Jewish New Year greeting.

meaning: See you tomorrow.
origin: Modern Hebrew farewell with temporal specifier.

meaning: With pleasure / no problem (literally 'in fun').
origin: Modern Hebrew slang; kef from Arabic kayf (mood, pleasure).

meaning: Join us / join in.
origin: Modern Hebrew imperative; reflexive of tziraf (to join).

meaning: Simply / it's simple.
origin: Modern Hebrew adverb; root p-sh-t (simple, plain).

meaning: It depends.
origin: Modern Hebrew; root t-l-h (to hang, depend).

meaning: How so / what do you mean?
origin: Modern Hebrew interrogative.

meaning: This is it / that's the thing.
origin: Modern Hebrew demonstrative reduplication.

meaning: Great / beauty / nice work.
origin: Modern Hebrew exclamation; yofi = beauty.

meaning: What an offer / what a deal — exclamation.
origin: Modern Hebrew exclamatory pattern.

meaning: There is no other choice — fuller form of ein breira.
origin: Modern Hebrew fatalistic formula; breira = choice.

meaning: It depends on me.
origin: Modern Hebrew first-person dependency.

meaning: I'll try.
origin: Modern Hebrew first-person future of hishtadel (to try).

meaning: That's how it is — fatalistic acceptance.
origin: Modern Hebrew fatalism formula.

meaning: There is nothing new under the sun; all apparently novel things are recurrences of what has happened before.
origin: Ecclesiastes (Kohelet) 1:9, attributed in the text to Kohelet (traditionally Solomon). One of the most quoted biblical phrases in both Hebrew and world literature, invoked to deflate claims of originality or to express world-weary cynicism about novelty. The Vulgate rendered it 'nihil sub sole novum', giving the phrase currency across European intellectual culture for two millennia.
why_it_matters: Instantly recognizable to any Hebrew reader. Knowing the Hebrew source helps decode allusions in Israeli public discourse and literature.

meaning: Vanity of vanities, all is vanity; human endeavors are ephemeral and ultimately without lasting value.
origin: Ecclesiastes 1:2, opening declaration of Kohelet (traditionally Solomon). Hevel literally means 'breath' or 'vapor' — something insubstantial that dissipates. The superlative construction havel havalim (vapor of vapors) is the Hebrew idiom for the absolute superlative, as in shir hashirim (Song of Songs). The phrase has become the archetypal statement of existential futility across world literature.

variants.note: First two words alone, used as shorthand for futility or impermanence.

meaning: To everything there is a season, and a time for every purpose; acting at the right moment matters as much as acting at all.
origin: Ecclesiastes 3:1, followed by the famous paired catalogue of opposites — a time to be born and a time to die, a time to plant and a time to uproot. Widely cited in Hebrew ethical literature to counsel patience and timing-awareness. Popularised globally by Pete Seeger's 'Turn! Turn! Turn!' (1959), which set the biblical text almost verbatim.

variants.note: Compressed form; used to indicate that the proper time for something exists and must be found.

meaning: A good name is better than precious oil; reputation outlasts material wealth.
origin: Ecclesiastes 7:1. Shemen tov (fine anointing oil) was among the most valuable commodities of the ancient Near East, used in ritual, cosmetic, and diplomatic contexts. Sa'di's Persian parallel (zaban sorkh sar sabz mi-dahad bar bad) makes the inverse point: careless speech destroys the good name. Together they frame the classical equation: reputation is priceless; speech is its primary threat.

variants.note: Two-word shorthand for a good reputation.

canonical: לא עליך המלאכה לגמור ולא אתה בן חורין ליבטל ממנה
meaning: It is not your duty to complete the work, but you are not free to desist from it; the obligation is effort, not guaranteed success.
origin: Pirkei Avot 2:16, attributed to Rabbi Tarfon (late 1st to early 2nd century CE). Addresses the anxiety of facing tasks too large for one person — legal, ethical, or communal. Applied today to education, social justice, environmental activism, and any long-horizon project.
why_it_matters: One of the most practically cited Talmudic principles in modern Israeli discourse. Encountering it in policy, education, or activism contexts is common.

variants.surface: לא עליך המלאכה לגמור ולא אתה בן חורין ליבטל ממנה

variants.note: First clause alone; used to excuse incomplete results without abandoning the obligation to try.

canonical: אל תדין את חברך עד שתגיע למקומו
meaning: Do not judge your fellow until you have reached his place; withhold judgment until you truly understand another's circumstances.
origin: Pirkei Avot 2:4, attributed to Hillel the Elder (1st century BCE to 1st century CE). The Hebrew makom (place) carries both physical and existential meaning — literally the location, but also the circumstances, history, and inner life. The injunction is not relativism but epistemological humility: you cannot know enough to judge. Functionally parallel to the English 'walk a mile in someone's shoes', but originating independently.

variants.surface: אל תדין את חברך עד שתגיע למקומו

variants.note: Compressed form; a warning against hasty judgment of others.

meaning: Who is rich? The one who rejoices in his portion; contentment is the true measure of wealth.
origin: Pirkei Avot 4:1, attributed to Ben Zoma (2nd century CE). He answers four parallel questions about wisdom, strength, wealth, and honor by inverting conventional definitions. Wealth redefined as contentment rather than accumulation is a cornerstone of rabbinic ethics. The full four-part teaching (eizehu chacham / gibor / ashir / mechubad) is among the most memorized passages of Avot.

variants.note: Predicate phrase alone: 'one who rejoices in his portion'; used as a character description.

canonical: אם אין אני לי מי לי וכשאני לעצמי מה אני ואם לא עכשיו אימתי
meaning: If I am not for myself, who will be for me? And if I am only for myself, what am I? And if not now, when? — the tension between self-reliance, collective responsibility, and urgency.
origin: Pirkei Avot 1:14, attributed to Hillel the Elder. Three questions in productive tension: the first mandates self-advocacy; the second warns against pure self-interest; the third demands present action. Widely applied to individual psychology, political organizing, and ethical awakening.
why_it_matters: The final phrase (ve'im lo akhshav eimatai — 'if not now, when?') circulates independently in Hebrew and English as a call to present action. Recognising all three questions reveals the full ethical balance Hillel intended.

variants.surface: אם אין אני לי מי לי וכשאני לעצמי מה אני ואם לא עכשיו אימתי

variants.note: Final clause alone; widely used as an independent call to present action.

meaning: Love your neighbor as yourself — the foundational ethical command of Leviticus, identified by Hillel as the entire Torah in brief.
origin: Leviticus 19:18. When a convert challenged Hillel to teach the entire Torah while standing on one foot, Hillel replied: 'What is hateful to you, do not do to your neighbor — that is the entire Torah; the rest is commentary; go and learn' (Talmud Shabbat 31a). The verse itself is the positive formulation; Hillel's negative restatement is the more widely cited teaching globally. Both carry enormous weight in Jewish, Christian, and humanist ethics.
why_it_matters: The most universally recognized verse of Leviticus. Recognising Hillel's commentary on it (Shabbat 31a) connects the Biblical command to rabbinic reinterpretation and the development of the Golden Rule across traditions.

meaning: A wheel it is that turns in the world; fortune is cyclical, and those who are high may fall as those who fell may rise.
origin: Talmud Bavli, Shabbat 151b, attributed to Rava bar Meri. Appears in a discussion on the obligations of the wealthy toward the poor: because the wheel of fortune turns, today's wealthy may be tomorrow's poor, so generosity is not just charity but prudent solidarity. The image parallels the classical Fortuna's wheel. In modern Israeli Hebrew, 'galgal hozer' is used colloquially when fortunes reverse unexpectedly.

variants.note: Compressed modern form: 'the wheel turns'; used in everyday Israeli speech.

meaning: Proper conduct (derekh eretz) precedes Torah; good character and social ethics come before religious learning.
origin: Yalkut Shimoni (medieval midrashic anthology). Derekh eretz (literally 'the way of the land') encompasses basic human ethics, social manners, and practical wisdom. The teaching argues that Torah study divorced from ethical conduct is incomplete; one must first be a good person, then a scholar. Used in modern Hebrew to argue that practical decency trumps formal learning or religious status.
why_it_matters: Foundational in debates about Jewish education and civic life: what comes first, character or knowledge? Recognising this phrase in Israeli discourse signals an argument about the relationship between civic and religious values.

variants.note: The two-word phrase alone; refers broadly to basic decent conduct and social propriety.

meaning: Who is strong? One who conquers his impulse; true strength is mastery of one's own desires, not physical prowess.
origin: Pirkei Avot 4:1, Ben Zoma (same teaching as he_eizehu_ashir_hasameyach_bechelko). The yetzer (impulse, inclination) — specifically the yetzer ha-ra (evil inclination) — is a central concept in rabbinic psychology. It must be redirected rather than eliminated. The teaching anticipates Stoic and later psychological frameworks for self-mastery.

variants.note: Predicate phrase alone: 'one who conquers his inclination'; used as a character description.

meaning: Whoever adds, detracts; excessive elaboration on a rule or text distorts its meaning.
origin: Talmudic hermeneutic principle derived from analysis of Genesis 3. God told Adam not to eat from the tree; Eve quoted the command as 'do not eat and do not touch it' — the addition 'do not touch' was not in the original. When the serpent caused her to touch the tree without consequence, the prohibition on eating seemed equally doubtful. The principle extends to all legal and textual interpretation: faithful reproduction matters; additions corrupt. Also used in modern Hebrew: over-explanation undermines the point.

meaning: Say little and do much; promise sparingly and deliver generously — the integrity of deeds over words.
origin: Pirkei Avot 1:15, attributed to Shammai (1st century BCE to 1st century CE). One of three principles for right conduct alongside fixed Torah study and receiving everyone with a cheerful face. The emphasis on undercommitment and overdelivery is the inverse of empty boasting. The folk formulation 'less talk, more action' is its secular descendant.

meaning: All Israel is responsible for one another; collective moral responsibility — each person's conduct affects the whole community.
origin: Talmud Bavli, Shevuot 39a. The legal context is communal atonement: can an individual atone for a sin affecting the collective? The principle extends far beyond: it is the foundation of Jewish communal solidarity, mutual guarantee, and the obligation to intervene when a fellow member of the community sins or suffers. In modern Israel, arevut hadadit (mutual guarantee) is a central concept in civic and social policy discourse.
why_it_matters: Underpins the institutional structure of Jewish communal organizations globally. Recognising it in modern Israeli discourse — welfare, security, social cohesion debates — is essential for reading Israeli public life.

variants.note: Modern Hebrew abstract noun form: 'mutual guarantee/responsibility'; standard in Israeli civic discourse.
